sections.heading: Theming in Jetpack Compose
sections.content: Jetpack Compose uses MaterialTheme as its theming system — a composable that provides a consistent set of design tokens (colors, typography, and shapes) to the entire composable tree beneath it. Any composable inside MaterialTheme can read these tokens via MaterialTheme.colorScheme, MaterialTheme.typography, and MaterialTheme.shapes. Material3 (Material Design 3) is the current version, introduced with Android 12's dynamic color system and a refined visual language. Unlike the View system where theming required XML styles and attribute resolution, Compose theming is pure Kotlin and fully type-safe — the compiler prevents you from using non-existent color or typography tokens.

sections.heading: Why Material3 Theming?
items: Consistent design — all composables automatically use theme colors, typography, and shapes without manual styling
Dark mode — switch between light and dark color schemes by providing different ColorScheme objects
Dynamic color — on Android 12+, automatically generate a color scheme from the user's wallpaper
Type safety — color and typography tokens are properties on typed objects; typos cause compile errors
Single source of truth — change the theme in one place and it propagates to all components in the app
CompositionLocal — MaterialTheme uses CompositionLocal under the hood; you can provide custom themes for subsections
Customizable — override individual tokens without replacing the entire theme
Material You — Material3 implements Google's Material You design language used across Android 12+ system apps

rows.material2: primary, primaryVariant, secondary, background, surface, error + on-colors
rows.material3: Expanded palette: primary, secondary, tertiary, error, surface variants, tonal surfaces + on-colors
Supported on Android 12+ via dynamicLightColorScheme() and dynamicDarkColorScheme()

rows.material2: h1-h6, subtitle1-2, body1-2, button, caption, overline

rows.material3: extraSmall, small, medium, large, extraLarge, full
Updated variants: TopAppBar, NavigationBar, NavigationRail, BottomSheet, etc.

rows.material2: Legacy — use only for projects already on Material2

sections.heading: Setting Up a Custom Material3 Theme
sections.content: A custom Material3 theme is a composable function that wraps MaterialTheme with your app's specific color scheme, typography, and shapes. The standard project structure creates an AppTheme composable in a dedicated Theme.kt file. This composable determines whether to use the light or dark color scheme based on the system setting, optionally applies dynamic color on Android 12+, and calls MaterialTheme with the resolved tokens. All other composables in the app are wrapped inside AppTheme at the root.

sections.heading: Theme.kt Structure
items: Define a LightColorScheme and DarkColorScheme using lightColorScheme() and darkColorScheme() with your brand colors
Create the AppTheme composable with a darkTheme parameter defaulting to isSystemInDarkTheme()
Add a dynamicColor parameter (default true on API 31+): if (dynamicColor && Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.S)
Apply dynamic color: val colorScheme = if (darkTheme) dynamicDarkColorScheme(context) else dynamicLightColorScheme(context)
Fall back to custom scheme: else if (darkTheme) DarkColorScheme else LightColorScheme
Call MaterialTheme: MaterialTheme(colorScheme = colorScheme, typography = AppTypography, shapes = AppShapes, content = content)
Apply in MainActivity: setContent { AppTheme { NavHost(...) } }
Use Material Theme Builder (m3.material.io) to generate a complete color scheme from your brand color

rows.description: Main brand color — used for key action buttons, active states, FAB
rows.on_color: onPrimary — text/icons on primary color surfaces

rows.description: Softer tonal container for primary — used for chips, cards with primary intent
Complementary accent color — used for secondary actions and highlights
Softer tonal container for secondary — filter chips, selected states
Third accent color for contrasting elements — badges, decorative highlights
Error state color — used for error text fields, error messages, destructive actions
Page/screen background color — behind all other content
Component surface color — cards, sheets, dialogs, menus
Alternative surface for differentiation — text fields, chips
rows.on_color: onSurfaceVariant — secondary text on surface variant

rows.description: Border and divider color — text field outlines, list dividers

sections.heading: Using Theme Colors in Composables
items: Access colors: MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary, .surface, .onBackground, etc.
Background color: Modifier.background(MaterialTheme.colorScheme.surface)
Text color: Text(text, color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.onSurface)
Icon tint: Icon(icon, tint = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary)
Container color in Card: Card(colors = CardDefaults.cardColors(containerColor = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.surfaceVariant))
Button color override: Button(colors = ButtonDefaults.buttonColors(containerColor = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.secondary))
Surface composable automatically uses theme surface color and applies the correct on-surface content color
Never hardcode Color values in reusable composables — always reference MaterialTheme.colorScheme tokens

rows.use_for: Hero text, large headlines on marketing screens
Top app bar title, prominent labels
List item titles, emphasized body text
Primary body text, default text style
Button text, tab labels, navigation labels

sections.heading: Custom Typography
sections.content: Custom typography is defined by creating a Typography object and specifying TextStyle values for each of the fifteen type scale roles. Each TextStyle accepts fontFamily, fontWeight, fontSize, lineHeight, letterSpacing, and other text properties. To use a custom font, add the font files to the res/font/ directory, create a FontFamily referencing them, and assign the FontFamily to the TextStyle values in your Typography object.

sections.heading: Setting Up Custom Fonts and Typography
items: Add font files to res/font/: my_font_regular.ttf, my_font_bold.ttf
Alternatively use Google Fonts Compose library: implementation("androidx.compose.ui:ui-text-google-fonts:1.x.x")
Create FontFamily: val MyFont = FontFamily(Font(R.font.my_font_regular, FontWeight.Normal), Font(R.font.my_font_bold, FontWeight.Bold))
With Google Fonts: val provider = GoogleFont.Provider(...); val MyFont = FontFamily(Font(GoogleFont("Roboto"), provider))
Create Typography object: val AppTypography = Typography(bodyLarge = TextStyle(fontFamily = MyFont, fontWeight = FontWeight.Normal, fontSize = 16.sp, lineHeight = 24.sp))
Override only the roles you need — unspecified roles use Material3 defaults
Pass to MaterialTheme: MaterialTheme(typography = AppTypography, ...)
Access in composables: Text(text, style = MaterialTheme.typography.bodyLarge)

sections.heading: Custom Shapes
items: Create Shapes object: val AppShapes = Shapes(small = RoundedCornerShape(4.dp), medium = RoundedCornerShape(8.dp), large = RoundedCornerShape(0.dp))
Pass to MaterialTheme: MaterialTheme(shapes = AppShapes, ...)
Access in composables: Surface(shape = MaterialTheme.shapes.medium) { ... }
Card automatically uses MaterialTheme.shapes.medium — override per instance with the shape parameter
Use CutCornerShape for angular brand aesthetics: CutCornerShape(8.dp)
Use CircleShape constant for circular components: Modifier.clip(CircleShape)
Shapes apply to both clipping and background rendering — clip and background shape must match for borders
Component-level overrides: Button(shape = RoundedCornerShape(4.dp)) overrides theme shape for that specific Button

rows.description: Android 12+ feature that generates a five-color tonal palette from the user's wallpaper and applies it as the app's color scheme
Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.S (API 31) — falls back to custom theme on older devices
dynamicLightColorScheme(context) — generates a light color scheme from the wallpaper palette
dynamicDarkColorScheme(context) — generates a dark color scheme from the wallpaper palette
val colorScheme = when { dynamicColor && Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= S -> if (dark) dynamicDarkColorScheme(ctx) else dynamicLightColorScheme(ctx); dark -> DarkColorScheme; else -> LightColorScheme }
Some apps provide a settings toggle to disable dynamic color — pass dynamicColor = false to AppTheme

sections.heading: Dark Mode Implementation
items: Detect system dark mode: isSystemInDarkTheme() — returns true when device is in dark mode
Pass to AppTheme: AppTheme(darkTheme = isSystemInDarkTheme()) { content }
Define separate LightColorScheme and DarkColorScheme with appropriate color values
Dark scheme: use darker, less saturated surfaces; lighter on-colors for text and icons
Compose Material3 components automatically adapt to the active color scheme — no manual dark mode handling needed
Force dark mode for preview: @Preview(uiMode = UI_MODE_NIGHT_YES) @Composable fun DarkPreview() { AppTheme(darkTheme = true) { MyComposable() } }
App-level dark mode toggle: store preference in DataStore; pass stored value to AppTheme instead of isSystemInDarkTheme()
Status bar and navigation bar colors: use WindowCompat and accompanist-systemuicontroller or Activity's enableEdgeToEdge() to match theme

rows.description: A mechanism to pass values implicitly through the composable tree without explicit parameter passing
rows.example: MaterialTheme uses CompositionLocalProvider internally for colorScheme, typography, and shapes

rows.description: val LocalSpacing = compositionLocalOf { Spacing() } — provides a default value
rows.example: data class Spacing(val small: Dp = 8.dp, val medium: Dp = 16.dp, val large: Dp = 32.dp)

rows.description: CompositionLocalProvider(LocalSpacing provides customSpacing) { content() }
rows.example: Wrap in AppTheme to provide custom spacing globally

rows.description: val spacing = LocalSpacing.current
Use when the value rarely changes — triggers full recomposition of the entire subtree if changed; more efficient when stable

sections.heading: Extending MaterialTheme with Custom Tokens
items: Create a custom extension object: object AppThemeExt { val LocalExtraColors = compositionLocalOf { ExtraColors() } }
Define ExtraColors data class: data class ExtraColors(val success: Color = Color(0xFF4CAF50), val warning: Color = Color(0xFFFFC107))
Provide in AppTheme: CompositionLocalProvider(AppThemeExt.LocalExtraColors provides ExtraColors()) { MaterialTheme(...) { content() } }
Add extension property on MaterialTheme: val MaterialTheme.extraColors: ExtraColors @Composable get() = AppThemeExt.LocalExtraColors.current
Access in composables: val success = MaterialTheme.extraColors.success
Use this pattern for brand-specific tokens not covered by Material3: success, warning, brand accent
Define light and dark variants of custom colors and switch them based on darkTheme parameter
Keep custom tokens minimal — use Material3 roles as much as possible before adding custom ones

rows.mistake: Hardcoding Color values in reusable composables
rows.fix: Always use MaterialTheme.colorScheme tokens — hardcoded colors break dark mode and theming

rows.mistake: Using Material2 and Material3 components together
rows.fix: Never mix the two libraries in the same project — they have incompatible theming systems and cause visual conflicts
Wrap preview content in AppTheme { } to see correct colors and typography in the preview panel
MaterialTheme automatically provides the correct content color (onPrimary, onSurface, etc.) via CompositionLocal — use LocalContentColor.current instead of hardcoding text colors

rows.mistake: Not providing both light and dark color schemes
rows.fix: Always define both LightColorScheme and DarkColorScheme — a missing dark scheme means dark mode uses light colors

rows.mistake: Applying dynamic color without an API level check
rows.fix: Always guard dynamic color with Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.S — it crashes on older devices

rows.mistake: Defining TextStyle font sizes in px instead of sp
rows.fix: Always use sp for text sizes in TextStyle — sp respects the user's system font size accessibility preference

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Material3 theming in Jetpack Compose provides a complete, type-safe design token system for colors, typography, and shapes that propagates automatically through the entire composable tree via MaterialTheme. Define light and dark ColorScheme objects with your brand colors, a Typography object with your custom fonts, and a Shapes object with your brand radii — then wrap your app in an AppTheme composable that selects the appropriate scheme. Take advantage of dynamic color on Android 12+ for Material You integration, extend the theme with custom CompositionLocal values for app-specific tokens, and always reference MaterialTheme tokens rather than hardcoding values to ensure consistent, dark-mode-ready UI across your entire app.
